Okay, but first off, we have to answer a very important question and that is why does good composition matter? And you might say to that, "Well, good composition just looks good." And you'd be right. It just looks good. It's easy on the eye and you want to just sit there and look at that image. And because of this, it keeps your viewer looking longer at your photo. It keeps them engaged in the image where their eye kind of naturally flows from one part of the image to the other part of the image without being too abrupt or uncomfortable. And I like to think of this as energy, right? You want to keep the energy in that frame instead of allowing it to escape from that shot. And this will keep your viewer looking longer at that image. Now remember this idea of energy as well cuz I'm going to be talking about it more in this video. And the last reason, which is perhaps the most important thing, is because good composition enhances the story of your photo. We can use composition to enhance our subject or certain parts of our image to tell a much deeper story in that photo. And ultimately, that is the most important thing in photography. It's the story that your photo is telling. So with that said, here are five common mistakes that you might be making that might be detracting from these three things that make composition so incredibly important. Now the first one is using leading lines incorrectly. Now you've probably heard of this idea of leading lines before. Leading lines are lines that kind of guide us in and around the photo to keep that viewer engaged and keep the energy in the frame. But lines can absolutely crush your composition as well. And where we see this happening most is with horizontal lines that lead us out of the image instead of leading us in and through and around that image. Like in this shot for example, it's a beautiful shot, but instead of having the road converge onto the line in the middle, it kind of cuts out of the frame and then our eye naturally gravitates

the image instead of leading us in and through and around that image. Like in this shot for example, it's a beautiful shot, but instead of having the road converge onto the line in the middle, it kind of cuts out of the frame and then our eye naturally gravitates towards this horizontal line in the middle of the image which then pulls our eye out of the left of the frame and there's nothing really keeping us in that composition. And the same goes for this shot here. This is a cool shot, but my eye kind of naturally follows these lines out of the left side of the frame. Or in this shot of a train that I shot in Japan, when I saw this, I thought it would be such a cool photo, but instead of having lines that kind of lead me back into the image and keep me in that photo, the horizontal lines on the train just kind of pull me out on either the left or the right and there's nothing really anchoring that composition. Now contrast that with a shot like this. Yes, we do have horizontal lines in this image, but our eye kind of immediately locks onto these vertical leading lines which all converge back onto the main subject in the back. So instead of leading out of the image, they're leading us into the image and settling on one vantage point which is the giant rock in the back. And this was shot at Morro Bay, such a cool location. By the way, this shot and a lot of my other shots were all edited with my free preset pack. I'll put a link down in the description and you guys can download that completely free. Now leading lines are absolutely everywhere. We can find them in cities, we can find them in nature, and I think it's always really important for us to look at that composition and ask ourselves, are the lines in this photo leading us in and around that composition and kind of creating this natural sense of flow or are the lines leading us out of the image and just kind of sucking the energy out of that photo? That's a really important question that you should be asking yourself every time you're shooting. And building off of that idea of energy, that leads me onto mistake number two, which is not having a clear subject in your photo. If someone's viewing your photo and there's no clear subject in that image, their eye is just going to naturally hunt around that image looking for something to lock onto, but when there's nothing to lock onto, they're going to immediately lose interest and move on to something else. Having a really clear subject in your photo really anchors that composition and it makes it easy for your viewer to know what to look at when they're looking at your image. And that's important because

lock onto, they're going to immediately lose interest and move on to something else. Having a really clear subject in your photo really anchors that composition and it makes it easy for your viewer to know what to look at when they're looking at your image. And that's important because once again, it keeps the energy in that scene instead of allowing the energy to escape. When you have a very clear subject, the first thing that the viewer will see in that photo is that main subject. They'll start there and then they'll kind of hunt around the frame a little bit and look at all the other parts of the image and then once again settle back onto that main subject. And that's the flow that we want to capture with composition. Now there's a few ways that we can make sure our subject is nice and clear in our scene. We can go for a more clean or minimal composition, which is something that I often pursue in my photography, but we can also use things like lines, frames, or depth to really keep the focus on that main subject in our photo. Like in this shot for example, I used the boat here in the foreground not only to frame my subject, but to also help add a sense of depth to that scene to really keep the focus on that main subject. And the same goes for this photo as well. The tree just kind of naturally frames the subject. It also acts as leading lines that kind of converge onto the subject and it's also adding a sense of depth to that image as well. And in this last shot, I went for a more minimal composition by using a lot of negative space to allow that subject to really stand out in this photo. Now I think one of the biggest misconceptions with this tip is people think that I'm saying you can only have one subject, but that's not the case. You can totally have more than one subject, but the main subject in your photo should be very clear and then we can use secondary subjects to deliberately support that main subject. Not only does this look good and help balance out our composition, but secondary subjects can also greatly enhance the story of our photo as well. Like in this photo for example, the bridge is very clearly the main subject in this photo, but the secondary subject here in the birds just adds a ton of visual interest to this shot and it kind of helps balance out this composition a little bit more. Or in this shot, the main subject is very clearly the tower in the middle, but then we have all of these other secondary subjects in the form of these smaller towers that kind of help support that main tower in the middle and they also help balance out that composition. But if there's nothing anchoring you into that landscape photo, once again, your eye is going to hunt around and leave the frame and lose interest immediately. For example, this photo that I shot of a boat in the Komodo Islands of Indonesia, if this boat wasn't here, this photo would be super super uninteresting because there's just nothing that's kind of locking you into that scene. Now the next mistake I see a lot of photographers making is shooting compositions that feel heavily unbalanced. And one of the ways that I like to think about balance in composition is through this idea of visual weight, right? Imagine all of the elements in your scene have some sort of visual weight to them. Larger and darker objects are going to appear heavier in your composition and it's important for us to include other elements on the other side of the frame to kind of help balance out that shot. Like in this shot for example, I really do like this photo, but this bottom area just feels so heavy because it's so dark and a simple crop could really help balance things out and keep the focus in the frame. Now contrast that with a shot like this. Yes, that main subject, the bridge, is super super heavy in this image, but we're using the shadow of the bridge to kind of help balance out that image and add a little visual weight to that bottom right part of the photo. And the same goes for this image that I shot in Morro Bay. The bottom of this photo feels very very heavy because it's darker, it's denser, and then there's a lot of negative space in the sky, but we're really helping balancing out this composition with all of the birds flying up in the sky and they also almost act as a leading line that kind of lead you up to that top bird there in the top left-hand corner of the frame. So I think it's really important to think about this idea of visual weight and balance in a photograph. What are the things that are weighing down your composition and is there anything else in that scene that you can include in your shot

top left-hand corner of the frame. So I think it's really important to think about this idea of visual weight and balance in a photograph. What are the things that are weighing down your composition and is there anything else in that scene that you can include in your shot that's going to help balance out that composition a little bit more and make it easier for your viewer's eye to kind of naturally flow throughout that composition. Now, the next mistake on this list is focusing too much on the details and not seeing the bigger picture in your composition. If you're looking at the scene in front of you, and you're focusing too much on all the little details, you're going to miss a lot of compositional elements in that scene. And one of the things that I like to do when I'm out shooting is purposefully unfocus my eyes so I can better see the light, the shapes, and the colors in that scene. This allows you to break that scene down into its most fundamental building blocks, and then you can compose based on that. And when you start doing this, I think you'll really start to notice that there are a lot of things in your scene that are impacting your composition that you might not have seen before because you were too focused on the details in that shot. So, for example, when I started doing this, I started to notice that light in my scene was acting as a compositional element that I hadn't seen before. And so, I started using harsh light as a really core component to my compositions in a lot of my images. Harsh light can create these really strong diagonal lines of interest that kind of guide our eyes around the photo. They can also help highlight our main subject, and they just look super cool as well, and they play a big role in composition. Like in this shot, for example, I shot this in the small town of Lucca in Italy, and the really strong light position in the left-hand side of the frame kind of helps balance out this composition. It also helps frame our subject in this shot, and their shadows also act as a leading line, which once again got our eyes right back to that main subject. This is another good example here. This is a photo that I shot at sunrise here in Bali inside of a volcano. You're actually looking into the caldera of a volcano, and you can see the really strong light is just kind of cutting through this image and converging onto our main subject in the shot, which is that tree in the middle. And then we also have a bunch of little secondary subjects that are helping enhancing the story of this photo. I really love this shot. And the same goes for all of these other shots as well. shot, which is that tree in the middle. And then we also have a bunch of little secondary subjects that are helping enhancing the story of this photo. I really love this shot. Now, if you guys do want to learn more about lighting in photography, I did a super comprehensive tutorial on lighting, and you guys can click up there to watch that, and I hope you enjoy. So, in short, don't focus too much on the details. Yes, the details are important, but so is the entire scene around your subject as well. So, try unfocusing your eyes and really noticing all the shape, light, and color in that scene and how they impact the composition in your photo. Now, last but certainly not least is shooting too zoomed in or cropping too closely in your images. And I see this so much with beginner photographers who think that the most important thing in their photo is the main subject, and nothing else in the scene really matters. But this could not be further from the truth for a few main reasons. The first of which is if you're cropping too closely or you're shooting too zoomed in, this can make your composition feel a little bit cramped, and it doesn't give your subject breathing room in that composition, which just makes the photo a little bit easier to look at. And also, if you're ignoring all of the things that are around your subject, you might be missing out on a ton of storytelling elements in that photo as well. So, I think the best way to explain this is to take a look at a few examples. So, with this photo, which shot do you think is more compelling? Is it the one on the left, or is it the one on the right? And hopefully, you said it's the photo on the right because the photo on the right has a lot of this really interesting negative space that not only helps frame our subject, but it also gives that subject a lot of breathing room in that scene. And on top of that, we can just see more of this beautiful beach in Indonesia, and I think it just leads to a much more compelling photo. The same goes for this shot here. This is a photo that I shot on the California coast. I was walking around, and I saw this really cool Coke bottle with the rose coming out of it, and it's just such a vibe. But if I had only focused on the subject, I'd be missing out on so much visual interest in the scene that's around it. All these different lines, and shapes, and colors that

All these different lines, and shapes, and colors that really make this shot much more interesting. Now, I'm not saying that you can't shoot zoomed in, or you can't shoot with a telephoto lens. Some of my favorite images were shot at 200 mm plus. You can still include elements around your main subject in that scene even if you're shooting telephoto. I just think it's really important for us to think about all the different elements in our scene and how they can enhance the story of that main subject, and also giving some space for that main subject to breathe a little bit in your photo. And that's why I really do like to use a lot of negative space in my photos is because it just makes the photo a little bit easier to look at. So, those are some of the main mistakes that I see a lot of photographers making with their composition. And as you can see, we talked about a lot of different compositional concepts throughout this entire video, but the one thing I want to say is that none of these concepts are rules. There's no right or wrong when it comes to composition or anything in photography for that matter. But there are preferences, and in my experience, some of the things that I've talked about in this video are things that a lot of people prefer to see in image, and that includes myself. But in no way am I saying this is the right way to do things and the wrong way to do things, but I do think that if you avoid some of these mistakes that I discussed today in this video, your composition will definitely level up.
